title: Stunting vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: [Stunting](#dod:stunting) measures the share of children younger than 5 years that are too short for their age. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Stunting vs. GDP per capita, 2024
Stunting measures the share of children younger than 5 years that are too short for their age. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between the share of children under five who are stunted and GDP per capita across countries in 2024. The vertical axis measures the percentage of children who are stunted, ranging from 0% to over 50%, while the horizontal axis plots GDP per capita in international dollars on a logarithmic scale from roughly $1,000 to $100,000. Points are color-coded by region, distinguishing North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. The data shows a negative relationship where higher GDP per capita coincides with lower stunting rates. Countries in the lowest income band, such as Burundi, Niger, and the Democratic Republic of Congo, cluster in the upper-left quadrant with stunting rates between 40% and 55%. In the middle of the distribution, nations like India, Pakistan, and Guatemala show stunting rates between 30% and 45% with GDP per capita figures around $5,000 to $10,000. Moving toward higher incomes, countries like Malaysia, Argentina, and Panama appear in the lower-right section with stunting rates generally below 20% and GDP per capita exceeding $20,000. The lowest stunting rates, falling below 5%, are visible in high-income nations including Australia, Estonia, and Belarus, where GDP per capita exceeds $50,000. The distribution forms a curve that descends from left to right, with the steepest decline in stunting rates occurring as GDP per capita rises from $2,000 to $20,000.
